He has also contributed to several CNCF-related projects, including serving as a reviewer for the system validator used in kubeadm preflight checks.

In 2023, he reviewed the Japanese translation of Learning eBPF, published by O'Reilly Japan. In 2024, he became an organizer of Cloud Native Community Japan (CNCJ), fostering open source culture in Japan. Additionally, he served as Co-Chair of KubeDay Japan 2024, playing a key role in shaping the event and promoting cloud-native technologies within the Japanese developer community.

Recognizing the growing importance of eBPF in cloud-native environments, Kenta founded the eBPF Japan Community for engineers across various industries to collaborate, share knowledge, and drive eBPF adoption in Japan.

Lee Calcote is the Founder of Layer5, where the community helps organizations harness and exploit the value of service meshes. Previously, Calcote stewarded technology strategy and innovation across SolarWinds. He led software-defined data center engineering at Seagate, delivering predictive analytics and modern systems management. Calcote held various leadership positions at Cisco, where he created Cisco’s cloud management platforms and pioneered new, automated, remote management services.
In addition to his role at Layer5, Calcote serves in various industry bodies, including as a SIG chair in Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), and formerly, participated in the delivery of Redfish 1.0 in the Distributed Management Task Foundation (DMTF), and Docker Benchmark 1.0 in Center for Internet Security (CIS). He is a startup advisor, Docker Captain, Cloud Native Ambassador, O’Reilly author, and speaker in the cloud native ecosystem.
Calcote is a maintainer of open source projects, including Meshery, Service Mesh Interface, and Service Mesh Performance. Calcote holds a bachelor’s degree in Computer Science, a master’s degree in Business Administration from California State University, Fresno and retains a list of industry certifications.
Meetup: Cloud Native Austin

When US funding cuts ended that path, I realized academia left me without agency.In the private sector, I discovered the value of risk. The Cloud Native paradigm let me experiment, fail fast, and try again without fear of lasting damage. In positive-sum collaborations, I saw how my algorithms could shape real-world outcomes, and how taking calculated risks created visible change for people and systems alike. This freedom to test ideas with relatively low cost revealed a new kind of agency, an immediacy and measurable connection to the meaning of work.My vision is to empower others to leverage cloud native technology with respect for the human element, security, and risk. Because having agency means creating reality for our society, for each one of us, one epsilon at a time.

person.name: Firat Kilic

I design resilient, self-healing, and observable systems that surface failures before they become incidents.  I focus on reducing complexity through automation and strong platform foundations, ensuring systems remain reliable under real-world pressure: traffic spikes, cascading failures and complex rollouts.My goal is to connect infrastructure and developer experience so teams can ship confidently, with reliability built in from day one.

person.name: Rodolfo Rivera López
person.bio: From the very moment I installed Red Hat 9 (without the Enterprise Edition), I knew open source was my calling. The challenge, the deep-seated need for knowledge to understand, the pure satisfaction of making something work, and then the drive to achieve much more—these forged my vision of technology. For me, every technical challenge is a designed opportunity to learn, execute, and iterate (not always in that order), a cycle that has given me powerful tools to deconstruct complex systems. This is my proven methodology for mastering what fascinates me most, a journey that has taken me through realms like GNU/Linux and BSD system administration, fullstack development, DevOps/Automation, Security and ultimately, the Kubernetes universe.  I believe technology defines us. Therefore, creating and applying it in the best possible way is, at its core, a contribution to a better society. This is why my commitment to learning is absolute and has no expiration date. It is the foundation from which I strive to reach the giants who built this wonderful open-source world, sharing whatever I can in return. Collaboration has proven to be the most viable path to the unprecedented; now is the time for all of us to contribute.
